coredata.dc:title: Resonance light scattering sensor of the metal complex nanoparticles using diethyl dithiocarbamate doped graphene quantum dots for highly Pb(II)-sensitive detection in water sample
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kaewprom, Chayanee
Sricharoen, Phitchan
Limchoowong, Nunticha
Nuengmatcha, Prawit
Chanthai, Saksit
coredata.dc:description: This study was aimed to detect Pb2+ using diethyl dithiocarbamate-doped graphene quantum dots (DDTC-GQDs) based pyrolysis of citric acid. The excitation maximum wavelength (λmax, ex = 337 nm) of the DDTC-GQDs solution was blue shift from bare GQDs (λmax, ex = 365 nm), with the same emission maximum wavelength (λmax, em = 459 nm) indicating differences in the desired N, S matrices decorating in the nanoparticles. Their resonance light scattering intensities were peaked at the same λmax, ex/em = 551/553 nm without any background effect of both ionic strength and masking agent. Under optimal conditions, the linear range was 1.0–10.0 μg L−1 (R2 = 0.9899), limit of detection was 0.8 μg L−1 and limit of quantification was 1.5 μg L−1. The precision, expressed as the relative standard deviations, for intra-day and inter-day analyses was 0.87% and 4.47%, respectively. The recovery study of Pb2+ for real water samples was ranged between 80.8% and 109.5%. The proposed method was also proved with certified water sample containing 60 μg L−1 Pb2+ giving an excellent accuracy and was then implied satisfactorily for ultra-trace determination of Pb2+ in drinking water and tap water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Resonance light scattering
Lead
Graphene quantum dots
Diethyldithiocarbamate

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of GQDs and DDTC-GQDs
Optimization of the RLS Probe for Lead Ion
Effects of Ionic Strength and Masking Agent
Method Validation of the RLS Probe
Characterization of the As-synthesized GQDs and DDTC-GQDs
Resonance Light Scattering Effect of Pb-DDTC Complex Associated With DDTC-GQDs
Effect of DDTC Concentration on Pb-DDTC Complex Formation
Effect of Amount of DDTC Doped in GQDs
Effect of Concentration of DDTC Doped GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR of GQDs (a), DDTC (b) and DDTC-GQDs (c).
The blue emission of GQDs (a) and DDTC-GQDs (b) under UV illumination.
EDX spectra of GQDs (a) and DDTC-GQDs (b).
(a) Absorption spectra of GQDs and DDTC-GQDs, (b) emission spectra of GQDs and DDTC-GQDs.

Absorption (red line) and RLS (black line) emission spectra of Pb-DDTC λem/λex = 553/551 nm in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ containing 1 mg L−1 of DDTC and 1.0 M acetate buffer pH 6.0 (slit width 3-3 nm).

RLS spectra of Pb-DDTC, Pb-DDTC-GQDs, and Pb-DDTC-(DDTC-GQDs) in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ containing 1 mg L−1 of DDTC, 100 mg L−1 of GQDs, DDTC-GQDs and 1.0 M acetate buffer pH 6.0 (slit width 3-3 nm).
Effect of DDTC concentration in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ and 1.0 M acetate buffer pH 6.0 (slid width 1.5–1.5 nm).
Effect of amount of DDTC doped in GQDs in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ containing 1 mg L−1 of DDTC, 100 mg L−1 of DDTC-GQDs and 1.0 M acetate buffer pH 6.0 (slid width 3-3 nm).
Effect of concentration of DDTC-GQDs in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ containing 1 mg L−1 of DDTC (slid width 3-3 nm).
Effect of pH in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ containing 1 mg L−1 of DDTC, 100 mg L−1 of DDTC-GQDs (slid width 3-3 nm).

Effect of incubation time of Pb-DDTC (black line) and Pb-DDTC-(DDTC-GQDs) (red line) in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+containing 1 mg L−1 of DDTC, 100 mg L−1 of DDTC-GQDs and 1.0 M acetate buffer pH 6.0 (slit width 3-3 nm).
Effect of concentration of NaCl as an ionic strength in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ containing 1 mg L−1 of DDTC (slid width 3-3 nm).
Effect of concentration of EDTA as a masking agent in the presence of 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ containing 1 mg L−1 of DDTC (slid width 3-3 nm).
Linearity of Pb2+ in ppb drawn from the RLSI of Pb-DDTC-(DDTC-GQDs).
Schematic illustration of resonance light scattering sensor of DDTC-GQDs for Pb2+ detection.
The tolerance limits and their recoveries of Pb ion a in the presence of interfering ions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration of lead ion used was 4 μg L−1 )1.93 × 10−8 mol L−1).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Pb2+ with several materials and techniques.
The Pb2+ contents and its recoveries of the water samples using the DDTC-GQDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: t exp. = 2.45 < t crit. = 2.78 for 95% confidence limit (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Resonance light scattering sensor of the metal complex nanoparticles using diethyl dithiocarbamate doped graphene quantum dots for highly Pb(II)-sensitive detection in water sample

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Chemistry Research Center, Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen 40002, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials Chemistry Research Unit, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Nakhon Si Thammarat Rajabhat University, Nakhon Si Thammarat 80280, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study was aimed to detect Pb2+ using diethyl dithiocarbamate-doped graphene quantum dots (DDTC-GQDs) based pyrolysis of citric acid. The excitation maximum wavelength (λmax, ex = 337 nm) of the DDTC-GQDs solution was blue shift from bare GQDs (λmax, ex = 365 nm), with the same emission maximum wavelength (λmax, em = 459 nm) indicating differences in the desired N, S matrices decorating in the nanoparticles. Their resonance light scattering intensities were peaked at the same λmax, ex/em = 551/553 nm without any background effect of both ionic strength and masking agent. Under optimal conditions, the linear range was 1.0–10.0 μg L−1 (R2 = 0.9899), limit of detection was 0.8 μg L−1 and limit of quantification was 1.5 μg L−1. The precision, expressed as the relative standard deviations, for intra-day and inter-day analyses was 0.87% and 4.47%, respectively. The recovery study of Pb2+ for real water samples was ranged between 80.8% and 109.5%. The proposed method was also proved with certified water sample containing 60 μg L−1 Pb2+ giving an excellent accuracy and was then implied satisfactorily for ultra-trace determination of Pb2+ in drinking water and tap water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Among different environment from various industrial activities including metal plating, oil refining and battery manufacturing, lead ion (Pb2+) can be taken into the body via inhalation, ingestion or skin adsorption. When the body is exposed to lead, it can be accumulated in the human body throughout the lifetime attributing its cumulative serious poison effects [1–5]. Water quality has become an issue of vital importance which is an essential resource that has been threatened by pollution. In drinking water, maximum allowable limit of total Pb of 50 μg L−1 is considered safe by World Health Organization, whereas <15 μg L−1 is adopted by the United States Environmental Protection Agency [6]. Therefore, it is important to develop a simple, fast and convenient method for lead detection, especially at ultra-trace level.

Several methods have been applied for lead determination including flame atomic absorption, inductively coupled plasma - atomic emission spectrometry, spectrophotometric methods and electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry [7–12]. However, these methods have a greater cost, higher sample volume requirements and instrumentation complexity limiting. Therefore, this study focuses on the determination of lead in water sample detected by resonance light scattering technique. Resonance light scattering (RLS) is a special light scattering occurring when the wavelength of the scattered light is located at or close to the molecular absorption band. The spectral characteristics and scattering intensity are strongly influenced by the molecular size, shape, conformation and interfacial properties, which further provide favorable new information for the study of the interaction of biological macromolecules and the molecular recognition [13,14]. In recent years, RLS technique has been widely applied in the quantitative analysis of drugs [15,16], surfactants [17], proteins [18,19], heavy metal ions [20–22] and nanoparticles [23] because of high sensitivity, rapidness, simplicity and convenience.

Regarding this method, Pb2+ forms complex with DDTC which is a chelating agent with strong tendency to form stable heavy metal complexes [24]. Furthermore, use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as an auxiliary ligand and substrate has attracted much attention in lead-diethyl dithiocarbamate (Pb-DDTC) interactions. GQDs possess large surface area, large diameter, fine surface grafting using the π-π conjugated network or surface groups and other special physical properties [25–27]. It has the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at their edge enable them to display excellent water solubility and suitability for successive functionalization with various organic, inorganic, polymeric or biological species [28,29]. For these reasons, GQDs have attracted significant attention worldwide. Nowadays, it has been applied as sensor for detection of various target analytes in numerous kinds of sample such as ferric ion in water sample [30], free chlorine in drinking water [31], aminothiols in blood serum [32], glucose in human serum [33] and lead in tap water and drinking water samples [34], indicating that GQDs can be used as a promising sensor for the detection of not only metal ions but also non-metal ions in various real samples.

In this research study, a novel RLS probe for the determination of lead based on diethyl dithiocarbamate doped graphene quantum dots was presented. In the presence of Pb2+ the resonance light scattering intensity of the DDTC-GQDs increases linearly by an enhancement mechanism. The interaction between Pb2+and the nanoparticle ligand leads to the aggregation of the particles to form bigger volumes of the complexes. This is aimed of using the RLS probe for determination of lead. In addition, the optimum conditions including each of DDTC and GQDs concentration, pH of the solution, ionic strength, masking agent, interfering ions were investigated in details. The developed method was applied for lead determination in water samples.

All chemicals used are of analytical grade including lead(II) nitrate (Fluka Chemika, Switzerland), citric acid (Carlo Erba, Italy), sodium diethyl dithiocarbamate (Sigma Aldrich, Switzerland), sodium hydroxide (Carlo Erba, Italy), sodium chloride (Ajex Finechem Australia), acetic acid (Merck, Germany) and sodium acetate (QRec, New Zealand. Potassium dihydrogen phosphate and dipotassium hydrogen phosphate was from LOBA (India). Paraffin oil was purchased from (Ajex Finechem Australia). Deionized water (Simplicity Water Purification System, Model Simplicity 185, Millipore, U.S.A.) was used throughout the experiment.

Spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC, Japan) with excitation and emission slit widths of 3 nm was mainly used. pH meter (Model Proline B21, Becthai Equipment & Chemical, Thailand), analytical balance (Model BSA224S-CW, Scientific Promotion, Thailand), quartz cell with 1-cm path length (Fisher Scientific, U.S.A.) were also used. Round bottom flask (Pyrex®, England) and hot plate with a magnetic stirrer in association with paraffin oil bath were set for citric acid pyrolysis method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of GQDs and DDTC-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots were prepared by citric acid pyrolysis [35]. Briefly, 2.0 g citric acid was transferred into a 100 mL round bottom flask which was heated to 250 °C in an oil bath for about 5 min. Citric acid was liquated and its color was changed to yellow. This liquid was then dissolved by dropwise addition of a sodium hydroxide solution (0.25 M, 100 mL NaOH) with continuous stirring for 30 min. The obtained GQDs solution was stored at 4 °C before use. For DDTC doped GQDs synthesis, the GQDs as prepared via citric acid pyrolysis were simultaneously treated with 1% (w/w) DDTC, 2.0 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of DDTC was transferred into a 100 mL round bottom flask, which was heated to 250 °C in an oil bath for about 5 min and then dissolved in NaOH solution as mentioned above. The DDTC-GQDs solution was also stored at 4 °C before use.

For RLS measurement, 0.5 mL solution of 1000 mg L−1 1% DDTC-GQDs was mixed with 0.25 mL of 20 mg L−1 of DDTC and 0.1 M NaCl in 1.0 M acetate buffer solution at pH 6.0. Various concentrations of Pb2+ were added into the solution mixture and adjusted to 5 mL with the buffer solution. The RLS intensities of the mixture solutions were recorded at 553 nm with fixed excitation and emission slit widths of the spectrofluorophotometer used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the RLS Probe for Lead Ion
ce:section.ce:para: For optimum conditions of the proposed method, the experimental parameters affecting the RLS intensity were investigated in detail including effects of the amount of DDTC doped in GQDs, the concentration of DDTC doped GQDs, solution pH, incubation time, ionic strength and masking agent.

Effect of the solution pH toward the RLS enhancement of Pb-DDTC-(DDTC-GQDs) was investigated by varying pH of the solution from 2 to 10 using acetate buffer and phosphate buffer solutions. The experiment was carried out by adjusting the two 1.0 M buffer solutions containing 100 mg L−1 1% DDTC-GQDs, 1 mg L−1 DDTC and 4 μg L−1 Pb2+ to the desired pH solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Ionic Strength and Masking Agent
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of ionic strength and masking agent on the RLS intensity of the system were investigated using NaCl and EDTA, respectively. The concentrations of NaCl and EDTA used were varied from 0.001 to 1.0 M NaCl and 0.001 to 0.5 M EDTA, respectively. The experiment was carried out by adjusting 4 μg L−1 Pb2+containing 1 mg L−1 DDTC to the desired concentrations of both ionic strength and masking agent.

Selectivity of the proposed RLS probe was investigated. The selectivity of Pb2+ detection was done in comparison with other cations according to some metal ions available in the regulation list of drinking water. Some cations were added into the reaction mixture including Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Cd2+ and Zn2+. The solution containing 4 μg L−1 of Pb2+ and various amount of the interfering ions was tested according to the recommended procedure. The tolerance level was defined as the maximum concentration of the foreign ion causing a change in the RLS signal not higher than ±5% RSD when compared with that of 4 μg L−1 Pb2+ alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method Validation of the RLS Probe
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed RLS probe was validated according to following analytical features: linearity, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantitation (LOQ), precision (% RSD), accuracy (% recovery) and statistics evaluation (t-test) of the tested results with the certified value. The background RLS intensities of either blank solution or control sample were normalized to minimize its noise signals <0.1% RSD.

To evaluate the applicability of the proposed method, the trace determination of Pb2+ in real water samples including drinking water, tap water and certified water was carried out. Tap water sample was collected from Khon Kaen province with polyethylene bottles and were analyzed without any pretreatment. All samples were filtered through a Whatman filter paper (No. 42) and adjusted to pH 6.0 with 1.0 M acetate buffer solution. In this regard, 1 mL of the water sample, 0.25 mL of DDTC (1 mg L−1) and 0.5 mL of DDTC-GQDs (100 mg L−1) solution were transferred into 10.0 mL volumetric flask. Then, each of the sample mixture was spiked with three concentration levels (2.0, 5.0 and 8.0 μg L−1) of the standard solution of Pb2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the As-synthesized GQDs and DDTC-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of GQDs, DDTC and DDTC-GQDs are shown in Fig. 1(a–c). From the vibration spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1(a)), the absorption peaks at 3261, 2977, 1563 and 1385–1136 cm−1 were observed, indicating the vibration modes of hydroxyl stretching, CH stretching, CC stretching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [36], CO in COH/COC (epoxy) groups [37], respectively. These results confirm the GQDs successfully synthesized by citric acid pyrolysis. The FTIR spectrum of DDTC is shown in Fig. 1(b), with the broad band at ~3300 and 1652 cm−1 corresponds to the bending mode of OH group of the adsorbed water. The absorption peaks at ~2900, 2073 and 1128 cm−1 indicate CH, SH and CS stretching [38], respectively. Surprisingly, the IR spectrum does not show the commonly found SH vibration of DDTC-GQDs at 2073 cm−1 (Fig. 1(c)). Rather, it shows the asymmetric stretching vibration of CS (1130 cm−1), CS (621 cm−1), respectively. More specifically, this SO 1065 cm−1 peak resembles the IR spectra in the previously reported sample of sulfer co-doped GQDs [39]. Fig. 2 shows photograph of the solution of GQDs (a) and DDTC-GQDs (b) taken under UV illumination at 365 nm. Comparison between both of them showed that the GQDs solution shows much brighter blue emission. The RLS is found to be independent on the blue emission of the solution, since the RLS mostly dependents on size, shape, conformation and interfacial properties of the nanoparticles. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was further used to ascertain the elemental compositions of the GQDs and DDTC-GQDs. Expectedly, the compositional elements; 45.3% O, 37.3% C and 17.4% Na were found in the pristine GQDs (Fig. 3(a)) and 38.9% O, 25.6% C, 25.7% Na and 9.8% S were obtained for the DDTC-GQDs (Fig. 3(b)). This further reaffirmed that the obtained DDTC-GQDs was successfully doped S bonding to GQDs. The Na line was the characteristic peak from NaOH solution used. According to the DDTC-GQDs, S (2.31 keV), C (0.277 keV) and O (0.525 keV) were occurred while a weak signal of N (0.392 keV) would be overlapped in between C and O. The fluorescence spectra of GQDs, DDTC and DDTC-GQDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The absorption spectra of GQDs and GQDs spiked with DDTC, Fig. 4(a), were both around 369 nm. When the DDTC was connected or decorated with the GQDs by pyrolysis method, the adsorption spectrum was blue shifted to 337 nm with an enhanced absorbance. While their emission spectra, Fig. 4(b), were around 459 nm. The report suggested the presence of intermediate surface states created upon functionalization with capping agent or heteroatoms (such as B, N, F and S) to be responsible for the excitation dependence [39]. It was, therefore, confirmed that the DDTC doped in GQDs was successfully obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Resonance Light Scattering Effect of Pb-DDTC Complex Associated With DDTC-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption and emission RLS spectra of Pb-DDTC are shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen, The RLS spectral of Pb-DDTC shows a peak as 551 nm when emission at 553 nm for all the investigated systems. After addition of GQDs and DDTC-GQDs to the Pb-DDTC, the RLS intensity was increased as shown in Fig. 6 . The reason could be explained in Scheme 1 . When the analyte interactions occur in between ligands leading to the aggregation of the nanoparticles to form bigger volumes of the metal complexes and their RLS intensities increase with the size of the nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of DDTC Concentration on Pb-DDTC Complex Formation
ce:section.ce:para: The DDTC concentration was optimized for the lead-diethyldithiocarbamate complex formation. The effect of DDTC solution on the RLS of Pb2+ was evaluated in the concentration range of 1–800 mg L−1 by spectrofluorophotometer with slit width 1.5–1.5 nm (Fig. 7 ). The results showed that the RLS intensity increases with the increase in DDTC concentration. The increasing of RLS intensity would be due to the association of DDTC with Pb2+ in the DDTC-GQDs nanoparticles. To minimize the amount of the reagent used, an excess of the DDTC solution (only 1 mg L−1) was, however, chosen for further experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Amount of DDTC Doped in GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For defining the optimal DDTC volume, various experiments were performed with different DDTC percent. The results showed that there was a considerable variation on 1.0 to 5.0% of DDTC by using the fixed concentration of GQDs at 2.0 g citric acid per final volume of the prepared solution. When increasing the amount of DDTC doped, the RLS intensity increased because the nanoparticles have been aggregation as shown in Fig. 8 . Accordingly, the 1% DDTC was chosen as the optimum one for the achievement of the method. The 1% DDTC was enough to detect Pb2+ at the ultra-level with lower volume of the reagents used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Concentration of DDTC Doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of DDTC-GQDs in fabricated RLS sensor plays an important role in the detection sensitivity. This behavior stems from size effect of DDTC-GQDs which light scattering is related to their size, shape, conformation and interfacial properties. Therefore, the RLS intensity will directly relate to DDTC-GQDs concentration. The concentration of DDTC-GQDs was varied from 0 to 1000 mg L−1 (Fig. 9 ) by controlling the solution pH at 6.0 of 1 M acetate buffer. The RLS intensities increase when the concentration of DDTC-GQDs increases. It is shown that the maximum intensity occurred when the concentration of DDTC-GQDs varied over this range. However, in considering the consistent manipulation, an addition of 100 mg L−1 DDTC-GQDs was chosen.

The pH solution is an important factor affecting the formation of Pb-DDTC-(DDTC-GQDs) complex. The effect of pH on the complex was studied in the pH range from 2.0 to 9.0. The results showed that the RLS intensity increases with increasing pH solution up to pH 6.0 and remains constant as shown in Fig. 10 . In this study, pH 6.0 of acetate buffer was chosen. The complex formation between Pb2+ and DDTC did not occur at pH below 6.0 because the DDTC is protonated in acidic solutions and can be decomposed into carbon disulfide and the protonated amine [14,40]. In addition, lead is mainly present as Pb2+ below pH of about 6. With increasing pH, the species PbOH+, Pb(OH)2 and Pb(OH)3 − become dominant over Pb2+ [14].

The effect of incubation time after mixing Pb2+ with DDTC and DDTC-GQDs was also evaluated as shown in Fig. 11 . The effect of incubation time was studied in the time range from 5 min to 60 min. The result shows that the RLS intensity of the reaction system can give higher intensity at 30 min. Therefore, the test should be completed within 30 min. The DDTC-(DDTC-GQDs) shows stable of an incubation time after mixing Pb2+ with different Pb-DDTC because the DDTC-GQDs can help the complex stable.

The effect of ionic strength on the RLS intensity is shown in Fig. 12 . The concentration of NaCl was studied in the range of 0.001–1.0 M. The results showed that the RLS intensity of Pb-DDTC complex keeps constant at 0.001 to 0.5 M and increases with 1.0 M of NaCl concentration. The variation of an ionic strength demonstrates that an electrostatic interaction plays a dominant role in this reaction [41]. With increasing of the concentration of NaCl, the effect of the electrostatic shielding of charges reduces the combination of Pb and DDTC, resulted in increasing RLS intensity. Therefore, this system should be suitably conducted under a low ionic strength condition, without adding NaCl, if need.

The effect of masking agent on the RLS intensity was investigated in the concentration of EDTA in the range of 0.001–0.5 M (Fig. 13 ). It was found that the RLS intensity of Pb-DDTC complexes keeps constant at 0.001 to 0.05 M and increases with 0.1 M of EDTA concentration. Therefore, this system should be carried out under a low EDTA concentration as a masking agent.

The effect of interferences in environmental samples on the determination of Pb2+ by the RLS method was studied. The interfering ions that would primarily be considered in drinking water for the determination of Pb2+ were investigated by adding their known concentration of each cation into the solution containing the analyte target. The results are shown in Table 1 . The effect of the transition metals had serious interfering on the determination of lead ion, but when using tartaric acid solution as the masking agent the tolerance levels of these cations could be satisfactorily increased. The tartaric acid was introduced and showed more masking efficiency than EDTA because this studied range of tartaric acid concentration had no any interfering effect of using for such masking agent at which the EDTA can form its complex with metal ion, and thus tartaric acid can be used as low concentration as a masking agent, in this case study.

The response of RLS toward a change of Pb2+ concentration was evaluated using the optimized condition. The RLS spectra of DDTC-(DDTC-GQDs) at different concentrations of Pb2+ are shown in Fig. 14 . Upon increasing Pb2+ concentration from 1.0 to 10.0 μg L−1, the RLS intensity linearly increases as a function of the increasing Pb2+ concentration. The RLS showed a good linear relationship between the RLS intensity enhancement and the concentration of Pb2+. The regression equation was found to be y = 85.78x [Pb2+, μg L−1] − 31.0 (R2 = 0.9899). Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were evaluated to demonstrate the limitation of the RLS. LOD and LOQ of the proposed RLS were 0.8 μg L−1 and 1.5 μg L−1, respectively. The repeatability and reproducibility were expressed in terms of relative standard deviation (RSD). The repeatability (intra-day precision, n = 3) and reproducibility (inter-day precision, n = 5) of the linearity slopes were also obtained. The intra-day precision and inter-day precision of which the percentage of RSDs were 3.74 and 8.13, respectively from the detection of 6.0 μg L−1 Pb2+. The results suggest that the proposed RLS exhibits very good repeatability and is therefore acceptable. Table 2 shows Pb2+ determination by several methods including FAAS, UV–Vis and fluorescence sensor. Interaction between Pb2+and DDTC leads to the aggregation of the particles complexes and tendency of rigid structure to get stable heavy metal complexes. In addition, Pb2+-DDTC can be determined via light scattering method which depends on size, shape and structure of the complexes and also has high efficiency and sensitivity (in ppb level). This work shows that DDTC-GQDs substrate for Pb2+ detection provides the lowest LOD of 0.8 μgL−1 (3.86 nM).

The proposed method was applied to drinking water and tap water for Pb2+ determination. Each experiment was performed in triplicate and the means of the results in terms of percentage recoveries were used for optimization and were calculated as follows:
(1)
Recovery
%
=
C spiked
−
C nonspiked
C added
× 100
where C
spiked , C
nonspiked , and C
added are the concentration of the analyte after addition of a known amount of standard in the real sample, concentration of the analyte in the real sample, and concentration of a known amount of standard that was spiked in the real sample, respectively [48]. From the results in Table 3 , the recoveries of spiked Pb2+ were obtained in the range of 80.80%–109.50% and based on student two-tailed t-test statistical evaluation results with a satisfying analytical accuracy of the measurement. The method was also evaluated by analyzing the certified reference materials (certified water sample). A comparison between the certified and the obtained concentration values was statistically no significant. It was concluded that the propose method can be successfully applied to water samples.

A highly sensitive resonance light scattering (RLS) sensor for detection of Pb2+ at the ultra-trace level using its metal-DDTC complex in association with DDTC doped GQDs was obtained. The ligand doped GQDs nanoparticles could linearly enhance the RLS intensity of Pb-DDTC and could be applied for the lead determination in real water samples. There is no trace amount of Pb2+ found in these drinking water samples tested, except for tap water samples ranging of 1.18–2.09 μg L−1. This novel approach can be implied for routine analysis for ppb level of Pb2+ detection under the optimum conditions with excellent analytical figures of merit and in-turn would serve as a friendly eco-environmental green chemistry.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Higher Education Research Promotion and National Research University Project of Thailand, Office of the Higher Education Commission, through the Food and Functional Food Research Cluster of Khon Kaen University, the Post-doctoral Program from Research Affairs and Graduate School, Khon Kaen University (60162), Materials Chemistry Research Center, Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Thailand for financial support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel rhodamine derivate as high selective detection lead sensor
